ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pork rib noodle soup; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pork rib noodle; A0FJP (noodle-based dishes) is the closest parent FoodEx2 category available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for pork rib noodle dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pork rib noodle as served in a Singaporean hawker centre typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising braised pork ribs, wheat noodles, broth, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Rib Noodle — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Pork Rib Noodle (排骨面), cr_id G00010088. All per-100g values are as reported. Note: the HPB record reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which reflects the pork rib component only and does not include the noodle component; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB source reports carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g for this record, which reflects the pork rib component only. Because the full dish as consumed includes wheat noodles, a GI of 52 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles (boiled), which is a low-GI food (GI ~52). Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100; since the HPB-reported carbohydrate for this record is 0.0 g/100g, GL is 0.0 for the data as recorded. Consumers should note that the noodle component adds meaningful carbohydrate load in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — critically exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit pork rib noodle due to its critically high sodium content of 1050 mg/100g; if consumed, they should request reduced broth, avoid drinking the soup, and use low-sodium soy sauce in preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: HPB data reflects pork rib component only (0 g carbohydrate); full dish with noodles carries significant refined carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should be aware that the noodle component adds substantial refined carbohydrate not captured in this record; request a smaller noodle portion or wholegrain noodles, and monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) and dietary cholesterol (89 mg/100g) combined with saturated fat from pork ribs
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit pork rib noodle due to its high sodium content, dietary cholesterol, and saturated fat from braised pork ribs, all of which contribute to elevated cardiovascular risk; portion size should be minimised and broth consumption avoided.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (pork) high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium 1050 mg/100g exacerbates joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit pork rib noodle as pork is a primary source of arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the high sodium content worsens RA-related tissue and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Rib Noodle (排骨面, Pái Gǔ Miàn) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean hawker dish consisting of braised or simmered pork ribs served over wheat noodles in a rich, savoury pork bone broth. The broth is typically seasoned with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and five-spice powder, giving it a deep umami flavour with a slightly sweet and spiced undertone. The pork ribs are tender from prolonged braising, and the noodles absorb the flavourful broth. The dish is commonly found in hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore and is a staple lunch and dinner option. Nutritionally, the HPB-reported data (171 kcal/100g, protein 19.1 g, fat 10.5 g, carbohydrate 0.0 g) reflects the pork rib component only; the full dish with noodles carries a significantly higher carbohydrate load. Sodium is critically high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The high protein content supports satiety and muscle maintenance. Clinically, patients managing blood pressure or cardiovascular risk should minimise broth consumption and request reduced-sodium preparation.
common_user_queries: Is pork rib noodle suitable for diabetics?
Can hypertension patients eat pork rib noodle?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010088), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. Confidence is high for all HPB-reported fields.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (cr_id G00010088); however, this value reflects the pork rib component only and does not include the noodle component of the full dish. The HPB database entry for this record reports 0.0 g/100g carbohydrate and 0.0 g/100g sugar, which is atypical for a noodle dish and is understood to represent the meat and broth fraction. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for Pork Rib Noodle (G00010088) as of 2026-04-16. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat noodles (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as the HPB record reports 0 g carbohydrate for the pork rib component; GL is 0.0 as calculated from the HPB-reported carbohydrate value.
